content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Let me start with something that has nothing to do with code.
You’re sitting at your desk working, and you need a pen. It’s in the other room, so you get up, walk over, grab it, come back, write one sentence, and put it back.
Five minutes later, you need the pen again.
So you do the whole thing again. And again. And again.
At some point — hopefully before the tenth trip — you’d stop and think: why am I doing this to myself? Just keep the pen on the desk.
That’s cache. Seriously, that’s the whole idea. Keep the things you keep needing somewhere faster to access so you stop making unnecessary trips.
So what actually is cache?
Cache is just temporary storage for data you’d otherwise have to fetch or calculate repeatedly. Instead of going back to the database (or an external API, or whatever your source is) every single time, you hold onto a copy somewhere faster or cheaper to access.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Without Cache vs With CacheThe database doesn’t have to answer the same question five hundred times an hour. It can relax. It deserves that.
Now, the first time you ask for something that isn’t cached yet, you still do the full trip. A cold cache doesn’t make the first request faster. What it does is make later requests faster, as long as the cached data is still valid.

Hit, miss — the two words you’ll hear constantly
When you fetch something through cache, the request ultimately results in either a cache hit or a cache miss.
Cache hit — the cache already has what you need. Fast. No database query is needed for that value. Everyone’s happy.
Cache miss — the cache has nothing useful. The app falls back to the database, gets the data, and usually stores it in cache so a later request can use it. The first request is slower; a following cache hit is much faster.
This is why caching matters a lot more at scale. If you have one user making one request, cache doesn’t change much. If you have ten thousand users all asking for the same product categories page, caching those categories can mean the database answers that query once instead of thousands of times.

The thing people forget: cache isn’t your real data
This trips people up more than anything else.
Cache is a copy. The original data source is the source of truth. Those are two different things, and they can get out of sync.
Think of it like this — you write “meeting at 3 pm” on a sticky note. The meeting gets moved to 4 pm. Nobody tells you. Your sticky note is now wrong, but it’s right there on your monitor, looking very confident.
That’s exactly what stale cache looks like in production. The application returns data quickly, the response looks completely normal, and the data is outdated. That’s arguably worse than being slow, because at least slowness is obvious.
So caching isn’t just a performance decision. It’s also a correctness decision, and sometimes a security one too — which I’ll get to.

TTL: giving your cache an expiry date
You can’t just cache something and leave it there forever. Well, you can, but you’ll regret it.
TTL — Time To Live — is how long a cached value should be trusted before the app goes back to the source for a fresh copy. Most cached data should have a TTL or another clear invalidation strategy.
What TTL makes sense depends entirely on the data:
Product categories → 1 hour (changes rarely)
Blog posts → 10 minutes (might get edited)
User permissions → very short TTL, with invalidation when access changes
Weather data → 5 minutes (changes constantly)Country list? Cache that for days, honestly. It changes very rarely.
A user’s account balance? Don’t casually cache that for an hour and hope nobody checks it twice. That’s how you get support tickets that are very unpleasant to read.
Longer TTL usually means fewer source requests, but potentially older data. Shorter TTL usually means fresher data but more database traffic. There’s no magic number — it depends on how much your data changes and how badly it matters if it’s slightly out of date.

What’s worth caching and what needs more thought
Some data is basically made for caching. It gets read constantly and doesn’t change that often:

• Navigation menus and product categories

• Blog posts and articles

• Public config values
Other data you need to think harder about before caching:

• Payment status or balances

• User permissions and roles

• One-time tokens (reset links, verification codes)

• Live inventory

• Anything medical or legal
The reason permissions are on that list isn’t performance — it’s that caching them for too long creates real security gaps. If someone’s access gets revoked but your cache still says they’re allowed in, that’s not just a performance bug. It’s a security problem waiting to happen.
Before caching anything in the second list, ask yourself a few things: how stale is too stale for this data? What’s the actual risk if it’s wrong? Who can access it, and should they be able to see a cached version?

Invalidation: where caching gets hard
This is the part nobody warns you about when you first start using cache.
Imagine a product is priced at 100 KM. It’s cached. Someone updates the price to 80 KM in the database. The cache still says 100 KM.
Now the source of truth and the cached copy disagree, and the one the user sees is wrong. The developer who owns that service is about to have a bad afternoon.
Getting data into cache is easy. Keeping cache in sync with reality is where the real work is.
The main approaches:

• TTL expiry — just let it time out and refresh. Simple, but you’ll always have a window where data is stale.

• Clear on write — whenever you update the database, also clear the related cache. Slower first request after any update, but way fresher data.

• Update in place — after writing to the database, immediately write the new value to cache too. Tricky to get right, but it can reduce the stale window.

• Versioned cache keys — include a version in the cache key and change that version when the data changes, so old cached values become unreachable rather than returned.
Which one you pick depends on how much staleness you can tolerate and how often your data changes. There’s no universal answer here. Data that changes a few times a month and data that changes every few seconds should not have the same caching strategy.

Before you reach for Redis, ask yourself a few things
Cache is genuinely useful. It’s also the kind of thing that gets added too eagerly and then becomes a source of subtle bugs for months.
Before adding cache to anything:

• Is this data actually requested enough to bother? (If it’s called once a day, caching saves almost nothing)

• How bad is it if the cached version is slightly wrong?

• What’s my plan for refreshing or clearing it?
If you can answer those clearly, you’re in good shape. If you’re adding cache because “it’ll probably be faster,” slow down and make sure you actually understand the data first.

Here’s what it actually looks like
Reading about cache hit and cache miss is one thing. Seeing it happen is better.
I put together a small CLI tool in Go that runs through the different scenarios side by side — direct database fetches versus fetches through cache. The data is a simple SQLite table of CRM pipeline stages, which is a realistic example of the kind of semi-static config data that gets read a lot and changes rarely.
Here’s how the cache itself is structured:
type CacheItem struct {
Data []Pipeline
StoredAt time.Time
ExpiresAt time.Time
}And the actual fetch logic, which is where all the decisions happen:
func fetchUsingCache() {
start := time.Now()
wasExpired := false
now := time.Now() if cache != nil {
if now.Before(cache.ExpiresAt) {
duration := time.Since(start) metrics.TotalReadRequests++
metrics.CacheHits++
metrics.TotalResponseTime += duration printResult("CACHE", "HIT", 0, duration, cache.Data)
fmt.Printf("TTL remaining: %s\n", time.Until(cache.ExpiresAt).Round(time.Second))
return
} wasExpired = true metrics.CacheExpirations++
} data := fetchPipelinesFromDB() storedAt := time.Now()
cache = &CacheItem{
Data: data,
StoredAt: storedAt,
ExpiresAt: storedAt.Add(cacheTTL),
} duration := time.Since(start) metrics.TotalReadRequests++
metrics.CacheMisses++
metrics.DatabaseReadQueries++
metrics.TotalResponseTime += duration cacheStatus := "MISS"
if wasExpired {
cacheStatus = "EXPIRED"
} printResult("DATABASE", cacheStatus, 1, duration, data)
fmt.Printf("Cache stored for: %s\n", cacheTTL)
}Three visible states in one function: hit, miss, and expired entry. That’s the full read lifecycle shown in this demo.
I added a 200ms artificial delay to the database fetch so the difference shows up clearly in a local demo. In real applications, some delay already exists naturally through query execution, network hops, or external APIs, although the exact timing varies.
Here’s what each scenario looks like:
Direct database fetch (no cache)
Source used: DATABASE
Cache status: NONE
Database read queries: 1
Response time: ~202msEvery request does the full round trip. Fine for some use cases, wasteful for data that barely changes.
First cached request (cache miss)
Source used: DATABASE
Cache status: MISS
Database read queries: 1
Response time: ~201ms
Cache stored for: 30sCache was empty, so the database still answered. But the result is now stored. Roughly the same speed as before, but this is the request that sets up everything after it.
Second request (cache hit)
Source used: CACHE
Cache status: HIT
Database read queries: 0
Response time: <1msThis is what you’re actually after. No database query is needed for this value. It is a very fast in-memory response in this local demo. The database didn’t even know this request happened.
After TTL expires
Source used: DATABASE
Cache status: EXPIRED
Database read queries: 1
Response time: ~202ms
Cache stored for: 30s30 seconds passed, so the cached value is no longer trusted. Back to the database — but the fresh data gets cached again, so the next request is fast again.
Database updated, cache NOT cleared
Source used: CACHE
Cache status: HIT
Database read queries: 0
Response time: <1msFast. Confident. Wrong. A new pipeline was added to the database, but the cache is still serving the old list. This is the scenario that causes real production incidents.
Database updated, cache cleared
Source used: DATABASE
Cache status: MISS
Database read queries: 1
Response time: ~203msSlower on purpose. The cache was cleared when the database changed, so the next request goes back to the source. One slower request to get back in sync, then later cache hits are fast again.
Full source code is on GitHub: cache-lab

Wrapping up
Cache is one of those tools that looks simple until you’re staring at a production bug where the database contains the correct value, the main application logic looks correct, and users are still seeing outdated data. Then it clicks — oh, the cache is still serving something stale.
The performance benefits are real, and they’re significant at scale. But cache also introduces responsibility. Every value you store in cache is a promise that you’ll handle it correctly when the underlying data changes.
The question isn’t “should I add cache?” It’s “Do I understand this data well enough to cache it safely?”
If the answer is yes, cache away.
